sentences.originalText: It was said that a new person had appeared on the boardwalk: a lady with a little dog.
sentences.simplifiedText: A new person was on the boardwalk: a lady with a dog.

sentences.originalText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov, who had been in Yalta for two weeks and was quite settled there, had started taking an interest in new arrivals.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov was in Yalta for two weeks and liked new people.

sentences.originalText: Sitting in Verney's pavilion, he saw a fair-haired young woman of medium height, wearing a beret, walking along the boardwalk; a white Pomeranian dog was running behind her.
sentences.simplifiedText: In Verney's pavilion, he saw a fair-haired woman with a white dog.

sentences.originalText: Later, he saw her in the public gardens and the square several times a day.
sentences.simplifiedText: Later, he saw her in the gardens and square many times.

sentences.originalText: She was always alone, wearing the same beret, and always with the same white dog; no one knew who she was, and everyone called her simply "the lady with the dog.
sentences.simplifiedText: She was always alone with her dog, and people called her 'the lady with the dog.'

sentences.originalText: " "If she's here alone without a husband or friends, it wouldn't hurt to get to know her," Gurov thought.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov thought, 'If she is alone, I should meet her.'

sentences.originalText: He was under forty, but he already had a twelve-year-old daughter and two sons in school.
sentences.simplifiedText: He was under forty and had a daughter and two sons.

sentences.originalText: He had married young, while still a student, and by now his wife seemed much older than him.
sentences.simplifiedText: He married young, and now his wife seemed older.

sentences.originalText: She was a tall, dignified woman with dark eyebrows, who considered herself intellectual.
sentences.simplifiedText: She was tall with dark eyebrows and thought she was smart.

sentences.originalText: She read a lot, used phonetic spelling, called her husband not Dmitri but Dimitri, and he secretly thought she was unintelligent, narrow-minded, inelegant, was afraid of her, and didn't like being at home.
sentences.simplifiedText: She read a lot, but Gurov thought she was not smart and did not like being at home.

sentences.originalText: He had started being unfaithful to her long ago—had been unfaithful often, and probably because of that, almost always spoke poorly of women, and when they were discussed in his presence, he would call them "the lower race.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov often cheated on his wife, so he spoke badly of women.

sentences.originalText: " It seemed to him that he had been so schooled by bitter experience that he might call them what he liked, yet he couldn't go two days without "the lower race.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought he knew women well, but he still wanted to be with them.

sentences.originalText: " In the company of men, he was bored and not himself, cold and uncommunicative; but with women, he felt free, knew what to say and how to behave, and was at ease even when silent.
sentences.simplifiedText: With men, he was bored and quiet, but with women, he felt happy and relaxed.

sentences.originalText: In his appearance, character, and whole nature, there was something attractive and elusive that drew women to him; he knew that, and some force seemed to draw him to them as well.
sentences.simplifiedText: He was charming and women liked him, and he liked them too.

sentences.originalText: Experience often repeated, truly bitter experience, had taught him long ago that with decent people, especially those from Moscow—always slow to move and indecisive—every intimacy, which at first pleasantly diversifies life and seems a light and charming adventure, inevitably becomes a regular problem of extreme complexity, and eventually the situation becomes unbearable.
sentences.simplifiedText: He learned that relationships with women could become difficult and complicated.

sentences.originalText: But at every fresh meeting with an interesting woman, this experience seemed to slip from his memory, and he was eager for life, and everything seemed simple and amusing.
sentences.simplifiedText: But when he met a new woman, he forgot this and felt excited.

sentences.originalText: One evening he was dining in the gardens, and the lady in the beret came up slowly to take the next table.
sentences.simplifiedText: One evening, he saw a lady in a beret sit at the next table.

sentences.originalText: Her expression, her walk, her dress, and the way she did her hair told him she was a lady, that she was married, that she was in Yalta for the first time and alone, and that she was bored there.
sentences.simplifiedText: Her look and walk showed she was a married lady, alone in Yalta for the first time.

sentences.originalText: The stories told of the immorality in places like Yalta are largely untrue; he despised them and knew that such stories were mostly made up by people who would have been glad to sin if they could; but when the lady sat down at the next table three steps from him, he remembered these tales of easy conquests, of trips to the mountains, and the tempting thought of a swift, fleeting love affair, a romance with an unknown woman, whose name he didn't know, suddenly took hold of him.
sentences.simplifiedText: He knew stories about Yalta were false, but he thought of a quick romance with her.

sentences.originalText: He beckoned coaxingly to the Pomeranian, and when the dog came up to him, he shook his finger at it.
sentences.simplifiedText: He called to her dog, and when it came, he shook his finger at it.

sentences.originalText: They walked and talked about the strange light on the sea: the water was a soft, warm lilac hue, and there was a golden streak from the moon upon it.

sentences.originalText: Gurov told her he was from Moscow, that he had a degree in Arts but worked in a bank; that he had trained as an opera singer but had given it up, that he owned two houses in Moscow.

sentences.originalText: And from her, he learned that she had grown up in Petersburg but had lived in S---- since her marriage two years before, that she was staying another month in Yalta, and that her husband, who needed a holiday too, might come to fetch her.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna said she lived in S---- and would stay in Yalta for a month.

sentences.originalText: She wasn't sure whether her husband had a job in a government department or under the Provincial Council—and was amused by her own ignorance.

sentences.originalText: And Gurov learned, too, that she was called Anna Sergeyevna.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov learned her name was Anna Sergeyevna.

sentences.originalText: Later, he thought about her in his hotel room—thought she would certainly meet him the next day; it was bound to happen.
sentences.simplifiedText: Later, in his hotel room, he thought about her and knew they would meet again.

sentences.originalText: As he got into bed, he thought how recently she had been a schoolgirl, doing lessons like his own daughter; he recalled the shyness, the awkwardness, that was still evident in her laugh and her manner of talking with a stranger.
sentences.simplifiedText: In bed, he remembered she was once a schoolgirl like his daughter, and he noticed her shy laugh.

sentences.originalText: This must have been the first time in her life she had been alone in surroundings where she was followed, looked at, and spoken to merely from a secret motive she could hardly fail to guess.
sentences.simplifiedText: This was her first time alone where people watched and talked to her for secret reasons.

sentences.originalText: He recalled her slender, delicate neck, her lovely grey eyes.
sentences.simplifiedText: He remembered her thin neck and her pretty grey eyes.

sentences.originalText: "There's something touching about her, anyway," he thought, and fell asleep.
sentences.simplifiedText: "She is special," he thought, and then he slept.

II

A week had passed since they had met.
sentences.simplifiedText: A week passed since they met.

sentences.originalText: It was a holiday.
sentences.simplifiedText: It was a holiday.

sentences.originalText: It was stifling indoors, while in the street, the wind whirled the dust around and blew people's hats off.
sentences.simplifiedText: It was hot inside, and outside, the wind blew dust and hats away.

sentences.originalText: It was a thirsty day, and Gurov often went into the pavilion, urging Anna Sergeyevna to have a drink or an ice cream.

sentences.originalText: One didn't know what to do with oneself.

sentences.originalText: In the evening, when the wind had dropped a little, they went out on the pier to see the steamer come in.
sentences.simplifiedText: In the evening, when the wind was calm, they went to the pier to see the steamer.

sentences.originalText: There were many people walking about the harbor; they had gathered to welcome someone, bringing bouquets.
sentences.simplifiedText: Many people walked at the harbor; they came to welcome someone with flowers.

sentences.originalText: And two peculiarities of a well-dressed Yalta crowd were very conspicuous: the elderly ladies were dressed like young ones, and there were many generals.
sentences.simplifiedText: In Yalta, old ladies dressed like young ones, and there were many generals.

sentences.originalText: Due to the rough sea, the steamer arrived late, after the sun had set, and it took a long time turning about before it reached the pier.
sentences.simplifiedText: The sea was rough, so the steamer came late after sunset and took a long time to reach the pier.

sentences.originalText: Anna Sergeyevna looked through her lorgnette at the steamer and the passengers as though looking for acquaintances, and when she turned to Gurov, her eyes were shining.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna looked at the steamer and passengers with her lorgnette, and her eyes shone when she looked at Gurov.

sentences.originalText: She talked a lot and asked disconnected questions, forgetting the next moment what she had asked; then she dropped her lorgnette in the crowd.
sentences.simplifiedText: She talked a lot and asked questions, but she forgot them quickly; then she dropped her lorgnette.

sentences.originalText: The festive crowd began to disperse; it was too dark to see people's faces.

sentences.originalText: " From the past, he preserved memories of careless, good-natured women, who loved cheerfully and were grateful to him for the happiness he gave them, however brief it might be; and of women like his wife who loved without any genuine feeling, with superfluous phrases, affectedly, hysterically, with an expression that suggested it was not love nor passion, but something more significant; and of two or three others, very beautiful, cold women, on whose faces he had caught a glimpse of a rapacious expression—an obstinate desire to snatch from life more than it could give, and these were capricious, unreflecting, domineering, unintelligent women not in their first youth, and when Gurov grew cold to them, their beauty excited his hatred, and the lace on their linen seemed to him like scales.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov remembered many women from his past.

sentences.originalText: But in this case, there was still the shyness, the awkwardness of inexperienced youth, an awkward feeling; and there was a sense of consternation as though someone had suddenly knocked at the door.

sentences.simplifiedText: There were also beautiful women who wanted more from life.

sentences.originalText: And in this constancy, in this complete indifference to the life and death of each of us, there lies hidden, perhaps, a pledge of our eternal salvation, of the unceasing movement of life upon earth, of unceasing progress towards perfection.
sentences.simplifiedText: This sound will stay the same even when we are gone.

sentences.originalText: Sitting beside a young woman who in the dawn seemed so lovely, soothed and spellbound in these magical surroundings—the sea, mountains, clouds, the open sky—Gurov thought how in reality everything is beautiful in this world when one reflects: everything except what we think or do ourselves when we forget our human dignity and the higher aims of our existence.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov thought everything is beautiful when we remember our dignity.

sentences.originalText: think of you," she said.
sentences.simplifiedText: She said, 'We are parting forever; it must be so.'

sentences.originalText: "God be with you; be happy.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna thought they should never have met in Yalta.

sentences.originalText: Don't remember anything bad about me.
sentences.simplifiedText: She said goodbye and wished him well.

sentences.originalText: We're parting forever—it must be so, for we should never have met.
sentences.simplifiedText: The train moved quickly and its lights disappeared.

sentences.originalText: Well, God be with you.
sentences.simplifiedText: Soon, there was no sound of the train.

sentences.originalText: " The train moved off quickly, its lights soon vanished from sight, and a minute later there was no sound of it, as though everything had conspired together to end as quickly as possible that sweet delirium, that madness.
sentences.simplifiedText: Everything seemed to end their sweet, crazy love quickly.

sentences.originalText: Left alone on the platform, and gazing into the dark distance, Gurov listened to the chirping of the grasshoppers and the hum of the telegraph wires, feeling as though he had only just woken up.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov stood alone on the platform and listened to the night sounds.

sentences.originalText: And he thought, musing, that there had been another episode or adventure in his life, and it, too, was over, and nothing was left of it but a memory....
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought about his life and felt it was just a memory.

sentences.originalText: He was moved, sad, and conscious of a slight remorse.
sentences.simplifiedText: He felt sad and a little sorry.

sentences.originalText: This young woman whom he would never meet again had not been happy with him; he was genuinely warm and affectionate with her, but yet in his manner, his tone, and his caresses there had been a shade of light irony, the coarse condescension of a happy man who was, besides, almost twice her age.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna was not happy with him, but he was kind to her.

sentences.originalText: All the time she had called him kind, exceptional, lofty; obviously he had seemed to her different from what he really was, so he had unintentionally deceived her....
sentences.simplifiedText: He was older and did not mean to trick her.

sentences.originalText: Here at the station was already a scent of autumn; it was a cold evening.
sentences.simplifiedText: The station smelled like autumn, and the night was cold.

sentences.originalText: "It's time for me to go north," thought Gurov as he left the platform.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov thought, 'I must go north now.'

sentences.originalText: "High time!
sentences.simplifiedText: He said to himself, 'It is time!'

"

III

At home in Moscow, everything was in its winter routine; the stoves were heated, and in the morning it was still dark when the children were having breakfast and getting ready for school, and the nurse would light the lamp for a short time.
sentences.simplifiedText: In Moscow, it was winter; the stoves were warm, and mornings were dark.

sentences.originalText: The frosts had begun already.
sentences.simplifiedText: The cold weather had already started.

sentences.originalText: When the first snow has fallen, on the first day of sledding, it is pleasant to see the white earth, the white roofs, to draw soft, delicious breath, and the season brings back the days of one's youth.
sentences.simplifiedText: When the first snow falls, the earth is white and nice.

sentences.originalText: The old limes and birches, white with hoar-frost, have a good-natured expression; they are nearer to one's heart than cypresses and palms, and near them one doesn't want to be thinking of the sea and the mountains.
sentences.simplifiedText: The trees look friendly, and they feel close to the heart.

sentences.originalText: Gurov was Moscow born; he arrived in Moscow on a fine frosty day, and when he put on his fur coat and warm gloves, and walked along Petrovka, and when on Saturday evening he heard the ringing of the bells, his recent trip and the places he had seen lost all charm for him.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov was born in Moscow; he came back on a cold day.

sentences.originalText: Little by little he became absorbed in Moscow life, greedily read three newspapers a day, and declared he didn't read the Moscow papers on principle!
sentences.simplifiedText: He wore his warm coat and gloves and walked in Moscow.

sentences.originalText: He already felt a longing to go to restaurants, clubs, dinner-parties, anniversary celebrations, and he felt flattered at entertaining distinguished lawyers and artists, and at playing cards with a professor at the doctors' club.
sentences.simplifiedText: He read newspapers every day and liked Moscow life again.

sentences.originalText: He could already eat a whole plateful of salt fish and cabbage.
sentences.simplifiedText: He wanted to go to parties and meet important people.

sentences.originalText: In another month, he fancied, the image of Anna Sergeyevna would be shrouded in a mist in his memory, and only from time to time would visit him in his dreams with a touching smile as others did.
sentences.simplifiedText: He could eat a lot of salt fish and cabbage now.

sentences.originalText: But more than a month passed, real winter had come, and everything was still clear in his memory as though he had parted with Anna Sergeyevna only the day before.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought Anna Sergeyevna would become a distant memory soon.

sentences.originalText: And his memories glowed more and more vividly.
sentences.simplifiedText: But winter came, and he remembered her clearly every day.

sentences.originalText: When in the evening stillness he heard from his study the voices of his children, preparing their lessons, or when he listened to a song or the organ at the restaurant, or the storm howled in the chimney, suddenly everything would rise up in his memory: what had happened on the pier, and the early morning with the mist on the mountains, and the steamer coming from Theodosia, and the kisses.
sentences.simplifiedText: When he heard his children or music, he remembered everything.

sentences.originalText: He would pace a long time about his room, remembering it all and smiling; then his memories passed into dreams, and in his mind, the past was mingled with what was to come.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov walked in his room and smiled.

sentences.originalText: Anna Sergeyevna didn't visit him in dreams, but followed him everywhere like a shadow and haunted him.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought about the past and the future.

sentences.originalText: When he shut his eyes he saw her as though she were living before him, and she seemed to him lovelier, younger, more tender than she was; and he imagined himself finer than he had been in Yalta.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna was not in his dreams, but he saw her everywhere.

sentences.originalText: In the evenings she peeped out at him from the bookcase, from the fireplace, from the corner—he heard her breathing, the caressing rustle of her dress.
sentences.simplifiedText: When he closed his eyes, she was there and looked lovely.

sentences.originalText: In the street, he watched the women, looking for someone like her.
sentences.simplifiedText: In the evenings, he felt her near him in the room.

sentences.originalText: He was tormented by an intense desire to confide his memories to someone.
sentences.simplifiedText: He looked at women on the street, hoping to see her.

sentences.originalText: But in his home, it was impossible to talk of his love, and he had no one outside; he couldn't talk to his tenants nor to anyone at the bank.
sentences.simplifiedText: He wanted to tell someone about his memories.

sentences.originalText: And what had he to talk of?
sentences.simplifiedText: But at home, he could not talk about his love.

sentences.originalText: Had he been in love, then?
sentences.simplifiedText: He had no one to talk to about Anna Sergeyevna.

sentences.originalText: Had there been anything beautiful, poetic, or edifying or simply interesting in his relations with Anna Sergeyevna?
sentences.simplifiedText: He wondered if he had truly loved her.

sentences.originalText: And there was nothing for him but to talk vaguely of love, of woman, and no one guessed what it meant; only his wife twitched her black eyebrows, and said: "The part of a lady-killer doesn't suit you at all, Dimitri.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri talks about love and women often, but no one understands.

sentences.originalText: " One evening, coming out of the doctors' club with an official with whom he had been playing cards, he couldn't resist saying: "If only you knew what a fascinating woman I met in Yalta!
sentences.simplifiedText: His wife says, 'Being a lady-killer is not for you, Dmitri.'

sentences.originalText: " The official got into his sledge and was driving away, but turned suddenly and shouted: "Dmitri Dmitritch!
sentences.simplifiedText: One night, Dmitri leaves the club with a friend after playing cards.

sentences.originalText: " "What?
sentences.simplifiedText: He says, 'I met a wonderful woman in Yalta!'

sentences.originalText: " "You were right this evening: the sturgeon was a bit too strong!
sentences.simplifiedText: The friend gets into his sledge and says, 'Dmitri Dmitritch!'

sentences.originalText: " These words, so ordinary, for some reason moved Gurov to indignation, and struck him as degrading and unclean.
sentences.simplifiedText: 'What?' asks Dmitri.

sentences.originalText: What savage manners, what people!
sentences.simplifiedText: 'You were right: the fish was too strong tonight!'

sentences.originalText: What senseless nights, what uninteresting, uneventful days!
sentences.simplifiedText: These simple words make Dmitri feel angry and upset.

sentences.originalText: The rage for card-playing, the gluttony, the drunkenness, the continual talk always about the same thing.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thinks about the boring days and nights with these people.

sentences.originalText: Useless pursuits and conversations always about the same things absorb the better part of one's time, the better part of one's strength, and in the end, there is left a life groveling and curtailed, worthless and trivial, and there is no escaping or getting away from it—just as though one were in a madhouse or a prison.
sentences.simplifiedText: He feels trapped in a life that is empty and unimportant.

sentences.originalText: Gurov didn't sleep all night and was filled with indignation.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov did not sleep all night, and he was angry.

sentences.originalText: And he had a headache all the next day.
sentences.simplifiedText: He had a headache the next day.

sentences.originalText: And the next night he slept badly; he sat up in bed, thinking, or paced up and down his room.
sentences.simplifiedText: He slept badly again and walked in his room.

sentences.originalText: He was sick of his children, sick of the bank; he had no desire to go anywhere or to talk of anything.
sentences.simplifiedText: He was tired of his children and his job.

sentences.originalText: During the holidays in December, he prepared for a journey, and told his wife he was going to Petersburg to do something in the interests of a young friend—and he set off for S----.
sentences.simplifiedText: In December, he told his wife he was going to Petersburg.

sentences.originalText: What for?
sentences.simplifiedText: But he went to S---- instead.

sentences.originalText: He didn't very well know himself.
sentences.simplifiedText: He did not know why he went.

sentences.originalText: He wanted to see Anna Sergeyevna and to talk with her—to arrange a meeting, if possible.
sentences.simplifiedText: He wanted to see Anna Sergeyevna and talk to her.

sentences.originalText: He reached S---- in the morning and took the best room at the hotel, in which the floor was covered with grey army cloth, and on the table was an inkstand, grey with dust and adorned with a figure on horseback, with its hat in its hand and its head broken off.
sentences.simplifiedText: He reached S---- in the morning and got a hotel room.

sentences.originalText: The hotel porter gave him the necessary information; Von Diderits lived in a house of his own in Old Gontcharny Street—it wasn't far from the hotel: he was rich and lived in good style, and had his own horses; everyone in the town knew him.
sentences.simplifiedText: The porter said Von Diderits lived nearby and was rich.

sentences.originalText: The porter pronounced the name "Dridirits.
sentences.simplifiedText: The porter said the name 'Dridirits.'

sentences.originalText: " Gurov went without haste to Old Gontcharny Street and found the house.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov walked slowly to Old Gontcharny Street and found the house.

sentences.originalText: Just opposite the house stretched a long grey fence adorned with nails.
sentences.simplifiedText: There was a long grey fence with nails in front of the house.

sentences.originalText: "One would run away from a fence like that," thought Gurov, looking from the fence to the windows of the house and back again.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov thought, 'A fence like that makes you want to run away.'

sentences.originalText: He considered: today was a holiday, and the husband would probably be at home.
sentences.simplifiedText: He knew today was a holiday, so the husband was probably home.

sentences.originalText: And in any case, it would be tactless to go into the house and upset her.
sentences.simplifiedText: It would be rude to go inside and upset Anna.

sentences.originalText: If he were to send her a note it might fall into her husband's hands, and then it might ruin everything.
sentences.simplifiedText: If he sent a note, her husband might see it and ruin everything.

sentences.originalText: The best thing was to trust to chance.
sentences.simplifiedText: The best plan was to wait for a chance.

sentences.originalText: And he kept walking up and down the street by the fence, waiting for the chance.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov walked up and down the street by the fence, waiting.

sentences.originalText: He saw a beggar go in at the gate and dogs fly at him; then an hour later he heard a piano, and the sounds were faint and indistinct.
sentences.simplifiedText: He saw a beggar enter the gate, and later he heard a piano playing softly.

sentences.originalText: Probably it was Anna Sergeyevna playing.
sentences.simplifiedText: Maybe it was Anna Sergeyevna playing.

sentences.originalText: The front door suddenly opened, and an old woman came out, followed by the familiar white Pomeranian.
sentences.simplifiedText: The door opened, and an old woman came out with the white dog.

sentences.originalText: Gurov was on the point of calling to the dog, but his heart began beating violently, and in his excitement, he couldn't remember the dog's name.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov wanted to call the dog, but he was too excited and forgot its name.

sentences.originalText: He walked up and down, and loathed the grey fence more and more, and by now he thought irritably that Anna Sergeyevna had forgotten him, and was perhaps already amusing herself with someone else, and that that was very natural in a young woman who had nothing to look at from morning till night but that confounded fence.
sentences.simplifiedText: He walked and hated the grey fence, thinking Anna Sergeyevna forgot him.

sentences.originalText: He went back to his hotel room and sat for a long while on the sofa, not knowing what to do, then he had dinner and a long nap.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought she might be with someone else, which was normal for a young woman.

sentences.originalText: "How stupid and worrying it is!
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov went back to his hotel room and sat on the sofa.

sentences.originalText: " he thought when he woke and looked at the dark windows: it was already evening.
sentences.simplifiedText: He had dinner and then took a long nap.

sentences.originalText: "Here I've had a good sleep for some reason.
sentences.simplifiedText: When he woke up, it was dark outside.

sentences.originalText: What shall I do in the night?
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought, 'What will I do tonight?'

sentences.originalText: " He sat on the bed, which was covered by a cheap grey blanket, such as one sees in hospitals, and he taunted himself in his vexation: "So much for the lady with the dog ...
sentences.simplifiedText: He sat on the bed and thought about the lady with the dog.

sentences.originalText: so much for the adventure....
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov thought about his new adventure.

sentences.originalText: You're in a nice fix....
sentences.simplifiedText: He was in a difficult situation now.

sentences.originalText: " That morning at the station a poster in large letters had caught his eye.
sentences.simplifiedText: At the station, he saw a big poster.

sentences.originalText: "The Geisha" was to be performed for the first time.
sentences.simplifiedText: The poster said 'The Geisha' was a new play.

sentences.originalText: He thought of this and went to the theatre.
sentences.simplifiedText: He decided to go to the theatre to see it.

sentences.originalText: "It's quite possible she may go to the first performance," he thought.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought Anna Sergeyevna might go to the play too.

sentences.originalText: The theatre was full.
sentences.simplifiedText: The theatre was very full that night.

sentences.originalText: As in all provincial theatres, there was a fog above the chandelier, the gallery was noisy and restless; in the front row, the local dandies were standing up before the beginning of the performance, with their hands behind them; in the Governor's box, the Governor's daughter, wearing a boa, was sitting in the front seat, while the Governor himself lurked modestly behind the curtain with only his hands visible; the orchestra was a long time tuning up; the stage curtain swayed.
sentences.simplifiedText: There was noise and fog above the lights, and people were talking.

sentences.originalText: All the time the audience were coming in and taking their seats Gurov looked at them eagerly.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov watched the people as they came in and sat down.

sentences.originalText: Anna Sergeyevna, too, came in.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna came into the theatre too.

sentences.originalText: She sat down in the third row, and when Gurov looked at her his heart contracted, and he understood clearly that for him there was in the whole world no creature so near, so precious, and so important to him; she, this little woman, in no way remarkable, lost in a provincial crowd, with a vulgar lorgnette in her hand, filled his whole life now, was his sorrow and his joy, the one happiness that he now desired for himself, and to the sounds of the inferior orchestra, of the wretched provincial violins, he thought how lovely she was.
sentences.simplifiedText: She sat down, and Gurov looked at her and felt love.

sentences.originalText: He thought and dreamed.
sentences.simplifiedText: He knew she was important and special to him.

sentences.originalText: A young man with small side-whiskers, tall and stooping, came in with Anna Sergeyevna and sat down beside her; he bent his head at every step and seemed to be continually bowing.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought she was lovely and dreamed about her.

sentences.originalText: Most likely this was the husband whom at Yalta, in a rush of bitter feeling, she had called a flunkey.
sentences.simplifiedText: A young man came in with Anna Sergeyevna and sat down.

sentences.originalText: And there really was in his long figure, his side-whiskers, and the small bald patch on his head, something of the flunkey's obsequiousness; his smile was sugary, and in his buttonhole, there was some badge of distinction like the number on a waiter.
sentences.simplifiedText: This man was tall and bent his head often.

sentences.originalText: During the first interval, the husband went away to smoke; she remained alone in her seat.
sentences.simplifiedText: He was probably the husband she called a flunkey in Yalta.

sentences.originalText: Gurov, who was sitting in the stalls, too, went up to her and said in a trembling voice, with a forced smile: "Good evening.
sentences.simplifiedText: The husband had a sugary smile and a badge.

sentences.originalText: " She glanced at him and turned pale, then glanced again with horror, unable to believe her eyes, and tightly gripped the fan and the lorgnette in her hands, evidently struggling with herself not to faint.
sentences.simplifiedText: During the break, the husband left to smoke.

sentences.originalText: Both were silent.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov went to Anna Sergeyevna and said, 'Good evening.'

sentences.originalText: She was sitting, he was standing, frightened by her confusion and not venturing to sit down beside her.
sentences.simplifiedText: She looked at him, turned pale, and was scared.

sentences.originalText: The violins and the flute began tuning up.
sentences.simplifiedText: The violins and the flute start to play.

sentences.originalText: He felt suddenly frightened; it seemed as though all the people in the boxes were looking at them.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov feels scared because people look at them.

sentences.originalText: She got up and went quickly to the door; he followed her, and both walked senselessly along passages, and up and down stairs, and figures in legal, scholastic, and civil service uniforms, all wearing badges, flitted before their eyes.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna goes to the door, and Gurov follows her.

sentences.originalText: They caught glimpses of ladies, of fur coats hanging on pegs; the drafts blew on them, bringing a smell of stale tobacco.
sentences.simplifiedText: They walk through halls and see many people.

sentences.originalText: And Gurov, whose heart was beating violently, thought: "Oh, heavens!
sentences.simplifiedText: They see ladies and smell old tobacco.

sentences.originalText: Why are these people here and this orchestra!...
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov's heart beats fast, and he thinks, 'Oh, no!'

sentences.originalText: " And at that instant he recalled how when he had seen Anna Sergeyevna off at the station he had thought that everything was over and they would never meet again.
sentences.simplifiedText: He wonders why people and music are here.

sentences.originalText: But how far they were still from the end!
sentences.simplifiedText: He remembers thinking they would never meet again.

sentences.originalText: On the narrow, gloomy staircase over which was written "To the Amphitheatre," she stopped.
sentences.simplifiedText: But they are not at the end yet.

sentences.originalText: "How you have frightened me!
sentences.simplifiedText: On the stairs, Anna stops and says, 'You scared me!'

sentences.originalText: " she said, breathing hard, still pale and overwhelmed.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna is breathing hard and looks pale.

sentences.originalText: "Oh, how you have frightened me!
sentences.simplifiedText: "Oh, you have scared me so much!" she says.

sentences.originalText: I am half dead.
sentences.simplifiedText: "I feel half dead now."

sentences.originalText: Why have you come?
sentences.simplifiedText: "Why did you come here?" she asks.

sentences.originalText: Why?
sentences.simplifiedText: "Why?" she repeats.

sentences.originalText: " "But do understand, Anna, do understand ...
sentences.simplifiedText: "Please understand, Anna," says Dmitri.

sentences.originalText: " he said hastily in a low voice.
sentences.simplifiedText: He speaks quickly and quietly.

sentences.originalText: "I beg you to understand....
sentences.simplifiedText: "I ask you to understand me."

sentences.originalText: " She looked at him with dread, with entreaty, with love; she looked at him intently, to keep his features more distinctly in her memory.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna looks at him with fear and love.

sentences.originalText: "I am so unhappy," she went on, not heeding him.
sentences.simplifiedText: "I am very unhappy," she says.

sentences.originalText: "I have thought of nothing but you all the time; I live only in the thought of you.
sentences.simplifiedText: I think about you all the time, Anna.

sentences.originalText: And I wanted to forget, to forget you; but why, oh, why, have you come?
sentences.simplifiedText: I wanted to forget you, but you came here.

sentences.originalText: " On the landing above them, two schoolboys were smoking and looking down, but that was nothing to Gurov; he drew Anna Sergeyevna to him, and began kissing her face, her cheeks, and her hands.
sentences.simplifiedText: Two boys were smoking, but Gurov did not care.

sentences.originalText: "What are you doing, what are you doing!
sentences.simplifiedText: He kissed Anna's face, cheeks, and hands.

sentences.originalText: " she cried in horror, pushing him away.

sentences.originalText: I beg you by all that is sacred, I implore you....
sentences.simplifiedText: "Please go away today, right now."

sentences.originalText: There are people coming this way!
sentences.simplifiedText: "I beg you, people are coming here."

sentences.simplifiedText: 'Do you hear, Dmitri Dmitritch?' she asked.

sentences.simplifiedText: Anna promised, 'I will come to Moscow.'

sentences.simplifiedText: She said, 'But now we must part.'

sentences.originalText: My precious, good, dear one, we must part!
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna said, 'My dear, we must part!'

sentences.originalText:
IV

And Anna Sergeyevna began coming to see him in Moscow.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna visits Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov in Moscow.

sentences.originalText: Once in two or three months, she left S----, telling her husband that she was going to consult a doctor about an internal complaint—and her husband believed her, and didn't believe her.
sentences.simplifiedText: Every two or three months, she leaves S----.

sentences.originalText: In Moscow, she stayed at the Slaviansky Bazaar hotel, and at once sent a man in a red cap to Gurov.
sentences.simplifiedText: She tells her husband she needs a doctor, and he believes her.

sentences.originalText: Gurov went to see her, and no one in Moscow knew of it.
sentences.simplifiedText: In Moscow, she stays at the Slaviansky Bazaar hotel.

sentences.originalText: Once he was going to see her in this way on a winter morning (the messenger had come the evening before when he was out).
sentences.simplifiedText: She sends a man to tell Gurov she is there.

sentences.originalText: With him walked his daughter, whom he wanted to take to school: it was on the way.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov visits her, and no one knows about it.

sentences.originalText: Snow was falling in big wet flakes.
sentences.simplifiedText: One winter morning, Gurov goes to see her.

sentences.originalText: "It's three degrees above freezing-point, and yet it is snowing," said Gurov to his daughter.
sentences.simplifiedText: He walks with his daughter to her school.

sentences.originalText: "The thaw is only on the surface of the earth; there is quite a different temperature at a greater height in the atmosphere.
sentences.simplifiedText: Snow falls in big, wet flakes.

sentences.originalText: " "And why are there no thunderstorms in the winter, father?
sentences.simplifiedText: "It is warm, but it is snowing," Gurov says.

sentences.originalText: " He explained that, too.
sentences.simplifiedText: He explained everything to her.

sentences.originalText: He talked, thinking all the while that he was going to see her, and no living soul knew of it, and probably never would know.
sentences.simplifiedText: He talked and thought about seeing her soon.

sentences.originalText: He had two lives: one, open, seen and known by all who cared to know, full of relative truth and of relative falsehood, exactly like the lives of his friends and acquaintances; and another life running its course in secret.
sentences.simplifiedText: He had two lives: one open and one secret.

sentences.originalText: And through some strange, perhaps accidental, conjunction of circumstances, everything that was essential, of interest and of value to him, everything in which he was sincere and didn't deceive himself, everything that made the kernel of his life, was hidden from other people; and all that was false in him, the sheath in which he hid himself to conceal the truth—such, for instance, as his work in the bank, his discussions at the club, his "lower race," his presence with his wife at anniversary festivities—all that was open.
sentences.simplifiedText: His real life was secret, but his false life was open.

sentences.originalText: And he judged others by himself, not believing in what he saw, and always believing that every man had his real, most interesting life under the cover of secrecy and under the cover of night.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought everyone had a secret life like him.

sentences.originalText: All personal life rested on secrecy, and possibly it was partly on that account that civilized man was so nervously anxious that personal privacy should be respected.
sentences.simplifiedText: People wanted privacy because they had secret lives.

sentences.originalText: After leaving his daughter at school, Gurov went on to the Slaviansky Bazaar.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov left his daughter at school and went to the hotel.

sentences.originalText: He took off his fur coat below, went upstairs, and softly knocked at the door.
sentences.simplifiedText: He took off his coat and knocked on the door.

sentences.originalText: Anna Sergeyevna, wearing his favorite grey dress, exhausted by the journey and the suspense, had been expecting him since the evening before.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna waited for him in a grey dress.

sentences.originalText: She was pale; she looked at him, and didn't smile, and he had hardly come in when she fell on his chest.
sentences.simplifiedText: She was pale and hugged him without smiling.

sentences.originalText: Their kiss was slow and prolonged, as though they hadn't met for two years.
sentences.simplifiedText: Their kiss was slow and long, like they had not met for years.

sentences.originalText: "Well, how are you getting on there?
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov asked, 'How are you there?'

sentences.originalText: " he asked.
sentences.simplifiedText: 'What news do you have?' he asked.

sentences.originalText: "What news?
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna said, 'Wait, I will tell you soon.'

sentences.originalText: " "Wait; I'll tell you directly....
sentences.simplifiedText: She said, 'I cannot talk now.'

sentences.originalText: I can't talk.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna could not speak because she was crying.

sentences.originalText: " She couldn't speak; she was crying.
sentences.simplifiedText: She turned away and used her handkerchief on her eyes.

sentences.originalText: She turned away from him and pressed her handkerchief to her eyes.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri thought, 'Let her cry and feel better.'

sentences.originalText: "Let her have her cry out.
sentences.simplifiedText: He decided, 'I will sit and wait for her.'

sentences.originalText: I'll sit down and wait," he thought, and he sat down in an armchair.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri sat down in an armchair and waited.

sentences.originalText: Then he rang and asked for tea to be brought to him, and while he drank his tea, she remained standing at the window with her back to him.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov asked for tea and drank it.

sentences.originalText: She was crying from emotion, from the miserable consciousness that their life was so hard for them; they could only meet in secret, hiding themselves from people, like thieves!
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna stood at the window and cried.

sentences.originalText: Wasn't their life shattered?
sentences.simplifiedText: Their life was hard because they met in secret.

sentences.originalText: "Come, do stop!
sentences.simplifiedText: They felt like thieves and were very sad.

sentences.originalText: " he said.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov told Anna to stop crying.

sentences.originalText: It was evident to him that this love of theirs wouldn't soon be over, that he couldn't see the end of it.
sentences.simplifiedText: He knew their love would not end soon.

sentences.originalText: Anna Sergeyevna grew more and more attached to him.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna loved him more every day.

sentences.originalText: She adored him, and it was unthinkable to say to her that it was bound to have an end someday; besides, she wouldn't have believed it!
sentences.simplifiedText: She adored him and did not think it would end.

sentences.originalText: He went up to her and took her by the shoulders to say something affectionate and cheering, and at that moment he saw himself in the mirror.
sentences.simplifiedText: Gurov held her shoulders and saw himself in the mirror.

sentences.originalText: His hair was already beginning to turn grey.
sentences.simplifiedText: He noticed his hair was starting to turn grey.

sentences.originalText: And it seemed strange to him that he had grown so much older, so much plainer during the last few years.
sentences.simplifiedText: Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov felt older and less handsome now.

sentences.originalText: The shoulders on which his hands rested were warm and quivering.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna Sergeyevna's shoulders were warm and shaking.

sentences.originalText: He felt compassion for this life, still so warm and lovely, but probably already not far from beginning to fade and wither like his own.
sentences.simplifiedText: He felt sad for this life, still warm and lovely.

sentences.originalText: Why did she love him so much?
sentences.simplifiedText: He wondered why Anna loved him so much.

sentences.originalText: He always seemed to women different from what he was, and they loved in him not himself, but the man created by their imagination, whom they had been eagerly seeking all their lives; and afterwards, when they noticed their mistake, they loved him all the same.
sentences.simplifiedText: Women loved him for who they imagined he was.

sentences.originalText: And not one of them had been happy with him.
sentences.simplifiedText: None of these women were happy with him.

sentences.originalText: Time passed, he had made their acquaintance, got on with them, parted, but he had never once loved; it was anything you like, but not love.
sentences.simplifiedText: Time passed, he met women, but he never loved.

sentences.originalText: And only now when his head was grey he had fallen properly, really in love—for the first time in his life.
sentences.simplifiedText: Now, with grey hair, he truly loved Anna.

sentences.originalText: Anna Sergeyevna and he loved each other like people very close and akin, like husband and wife, like tender friends; it seemed to them that fate itself had meant them for one another, and they couldn't understand why he had a wife and she a husband; and it was as though they were a pair of birds of passage, caught and forced to live in different cages.
sentences.simplifiedText: Anna and Dmitri loved like husband and wife.

sentences.originalText: They forgave each other for what they were ashamed of in their past, they forgave everything in the present, and felt that this love of theirs had changed them both.
sentences.simplifiedText: They forgave each other and felt changed by love.

sentences.originalText: In moments of depression in the past, he had comforted himself with any arguments that came into his mind, but now he no longer cared for arguments; he felt profound compassion, he wanted to be sincere and tender....
sentences.simplifiedText: In the past, Dmitri Dmitritch Gurov felt sad and used arguments to feel better.

sentences.originalText: "Don't cry, my darling," he said.
sentences.simplifiedText: Now, he does not care for arguments and wants to be kind.

sentences.originalText: "You've had your cry; that's enough....
sentences.simplifiedText: "Don't cry, my darling," he said to Anna Sergeyevna.

sentences.originalText: Let's talk now, let's think of some plan.
sentences.simplifiedText: "You have cried enough; let's talk now.

sentences.originalText: " Then they spent a long while taking counsel together, talked of how to avoid the necessity for secrecy, for deception, for living in different towns and not seeing each other for long at a time.
sentences.simplifiedText: Let's think of a plan together," he said.

sentences.originalText: How could they be free from this intolerable bondage?
sentences.simplifiedText: They talked for a long time about their problem.

sentences.originalText: "How?
sentences.simplifiedText: They wanted to stop hiding and live in the same place.

sentences.originalText: How?
sentences.simplifiedText: "How can we be free?" he asked.

sentences.originalText: " he asked, clutching his head.
sentences.simplifiedText: He held his head and thought hard.
